title: RFK Jr. Wants FDA to Review Every New Food Ingredient — Industry Is Already Threatening Lawsuits
description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is pushing to close the GRAS loophole that lets food companies self-certify ingredient safety. Here's what it means for your grocery cart.
lede: A 67-year-old regulatory shortcut lets food companies decide for themselves whether new ingredients are safe. The Health Secretary wants to change that — and the food industry is lawyering up.

sections.heading: The GRAS Loophole, Explained
paragraphs: Since 1958, the FDA has allowed food manufacturers to classify new ingredients as "Generally Recognized as Safe" (GRAS) without requiring federal review. The provision was originally designed for well-understood substances like vinegar and baking soda. Instead, it became a highway for thousands of novel additives to enter the American food supply with zero government oversight.

Nobody knows exactly how many GRAS substances are in circulation — estimates run into the thousands. Companies can hire their own scientists, conduct their own safety reviews, and add ingredients to products without ever notifying the FDA. It's the regulatory equivalent of grading your own homework.

Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has now proposed requiring mandatory FDA notification and review for all new food ingredients — closing the self-certification loophole that consumer advocates have criticized for decades.

sections.heading: Why the Food Industry Is Fighting Back
paragraphs: The pushback has been immediate and aggressive. Lawyers representing major food companies and industry trade groups argue that mandatory FDA review would cripple innovation. Their core complaint: the FDA moves too slowly, and requiring 100-page notification filings for every new ingredient would bottleneck the entire food development pipeline.

Todd A. Harrison, a Washington lawyer who represents food and dietary supplement makers, told the New York Times that if the FDA mandated filings, 'I think FDA loses that case' in court. Several industry groups are already preparing legal challenges before any official proposal has even been published.

The irony isn't lost on food safety advocates: the same companies that have self-certified thousands of ingredients as safe are now arguing they can't handle the paperwork of proving it to the government.

sections.heading: What's Actually in Your Food?
paragraphs: The average American consumes an estimated 3-5 pounds of food additives per year. Some common ingredients that entered the food supply through the GRAS pathway include:

Red 40 and Yellow 5 — synthetic food dyes linked to hyperactivity in children, banned or restricted in several European countries. BHA and BHT — preservatives classified as possible carcinogens by some international health agencies. Titanium dioxide — a whitening agent banned in the EU since 2022 but still legal in the US. Potassium bromate — a flour treatment agent banned in the EU, UK, Canada, and Brazil, still permitted in American bread.

The disconnect between US and international food safety standards has become a growing concern. The EU operates on a precautionary principle — proving safety before approval — while the US system often waits for evidence of harm before acting.

sections.heading: Where This Goes From Here
paragraphs: Kennedy's proposal is still in early stages. No formal rule has been published, and the notice-and-comment period required for federal rulemaking could take months or years. Meanwhile, the food industry is already signaling that any rule will face immediate legal challenge.

For consumers, the most actionable step right now is learning to read nutrition labels and ingredient lists. Understanding what's in your food — and which additives are under scrutiny — doesn't require waiting for Washington to sort itself out.

Tools like nutrition label analyzers and ingredient decoders can help you identify controversial additives in seconds. Whether or not the GRAS loophole gets closed, knowing what you're eating remains the first line of defense.

faqs.q: What is the GRAS loophole in food safety?
faqs.a: GRAS stands for 'Generally Recognized as Safe.' Since 1958, it has allowed food companies to self-certify new ingredients as safe without requiring FDA review or notification. Thousands of additives have entered the US food supply through this process.

faqs.q: What food additives are banned in Europe but legal in the US?
faqs.a: Several additives legal in the US are banned or restricted in the EU, including titanium dioxide (a whitening agent), potassium bromate (a flour treatment), and certain synthetic food dyes like Red 40 and Yellow 5, which face mandatory warning labels in the EU.

faqs.q: How can I check if my food contains controversial ingredients?
faqs.a: Read the ingredient list on the packaging and use a nutrition label analyzer tool to decode additives. Look for synthetic dyes (colors followed by numbers), preservatives like BHA/BHT, and artificial sweeteners. Shorter ingredient lists with recognizable whole foods are generally safer.

faqs.q: Will the FDA actually start reviewing all food ingredients?
faqs.a: Health Secretary RFK Jr. has proposed closing the GRAS self-certification loophole, but the formal rulemaking process takes months or years. The food industry is already preparing legal challenges, so the timeline and outcome remain uncertain.

faqs.q: How many food additives are in the average American diet?
faqs.a: Americans consume an estimated 3-5 pounds of food additives per year. The exact number of GRAS substances in the food supply is unknown, but estimates suggest thousands of additives have been self-certified as safe by manufacturers without FDA review.
